inputs.userGoal: Configure a site recovery plan for disaster failover from East to West Azure region, replicate major VM workloads, and automate DNS failbacks.

reasoning.whyChosen: Represents the primary East US network boundary hosting critical VM workloads that must be protected.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Existing topology is preserved for lower migration risk but may limit DR network simplification.

reasoning.whyChosen: Pre-provisioned target network is required for deterministic VM placement and fast failover execution.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Pre-provisioning adds minor standing cost but significantly reduces recovery time objective risk.

references.title: Prepare target Azure network for disaster recovery

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure Site Recovery for Azure-to-Azure VM replication is anchored on a Recovery Services vault.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Site Recovery adds configuration complexity but provides orchestration and low-RPO replication.

references.title: Azure Site Recovery architecture for Azure-to-Azure

reasoning.whyChosen: Site Recovery uses cache storage in the source region for replication staging and throughput smoothing.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard_LRS minimizes cost but can provide lower throughput than premium tiers for very high-change workloads.

references.title: Prepare source for Azure VM disaster recovery

resources.name: asr-policy-eus-wus

reasoning.whyChosen: Policy defines RPO/RTO behavior and enables multi-VM consistency for major VM workloads.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Longer retention improves rollback options but increases storage consumption and cost.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Replication policy is scoped to the Recovery Services vault.

references.title: Configure replication settings for Azure VM disaster recovery

reasoning.whyChosen: Traffic Manager provides automatic DNS-level failover and failback as endpoint health changes between East and West.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Traffic Manager is simple and cost-effective for regional endpoint failover but has DNS TTL convergence delay.

resources.name: dnszone-workload-contoso-com

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure DNS hosts authoritative records and delegates endpoint control to Traffic Manager for automated failback.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Low TTL improves switchover speed but can slightly increase DNS query volume.

resources.dependencyReasoning: DNS CNAME points to the Traffic Manager profile FQDN.

resources.name: func-dns-failback-eus

properties.purpose: Optional post-failover/failback DNS orchestration and validation

reasoning.whyChosen: Function App enables event-driven automation for controlled DNS failback checks and custom workflows.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Function App is flexible for custom logic but requires code lifecycle management.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Function needs RBAC access to update/read DNS records.

resources.name: evg-asr-events-eus

reasoning.whyChosen: Event Grid triggers DNS orchestration automation immediately after recovery milestones.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Event-driven design reduces manual delay but requires robust function error handling and retries.

resources.dependencyReasoning: System topic source is the Recovery Services vault and sink is the Function endpoint.

references.title: Route events from Azure services to Event Grid

overallReasoning.summary: The architecture prioritizes business continuity by combining Azure Site Recovery for VM replication with globally managed DNS failover/failback through Traffic Manager and Azure DNS, plus optional event-driven DNS workflow automation.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: This default plan favors resilience and automation over minimum cost; standing DR network and orchestration services add baseline spend but reduce operational risk during regional outages.

plan.validation: Plan is coherent for East US primary to West US DR with VM replication prerequisites, target networking, and automated DNS failover/failback workflow hooks.

references.title: Business continuity and disaster recovery in Azure
